---
title: "What Is an Optimization Problem?"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Optimization"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article,
# matched to the rest of the series.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

CURVE  = "#2b3a67"    # the objective curve / surface
MARK   = "#c1121f"    # the optimizer / highlighted point
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"    # feasible region / secondary highlight
GREY   = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the front door to the entire **Optimization**
> track, and it assumes nothing above the Calculus-I floor — functions, their graphs, and
> the idea of a largest or smallest value on an interval. It leans on no other Core
> Sample. Its whole job is to fix *vocabulary and viewpoint*: to turn the everyday phrase
> "find the best" into one precise mathematical object, so that every later article has a
> shared language. It sets up [OPTM-01 *One-Dimensional Minimization*] (the first actual
> *method* — how Calculus I locates a minimum) and, through that, the entire machinery of
> the track: stationary points, convexity, gradient descent, constraints, and duality.
> Almost every other track in the series eventually says "fit the model by minimizing
> *this*" and points back here for what that sentence means.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Across data science, the same four words keep surfacing: *find the best*. The best line
through a cloud of points, the best parameters for a model, the best allocation of a
budget. This article makes that phrase mean exactly one thing. An **optimization problem**
is a triple — a **decision variable** you are free to choose, an **objective function**
that scores every choice with a single number, and a **feasible set** of choices you are
allowed to make — together with the instruction *make the score as small as possible*. We
fix the notation `minimize f(x) subject to x ∈ C`, separate the **minimum value** from the
**minimizer** that achieves it (the difference between `min` and `argmin`, which trips up
more interviews than it should), explain why maximizing is the same problem in disguise,
and distinguish a *local* best from the *global* best. The single claim this article
defends is that **once you can write a problem in this form, you have already done the
first and most important step** — and that the cases where no best choice exists are not
exotic pathologies but the first honest lesson about what minimization can and cannot
promise.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
An **optimization problem** asks: among all choices $x$ that are *allowed*, which one
makes a chosen *score* smallest? It is fully specified by three pieces —

- a **decision variable** $x$ (what you get to choose),
- an **objective** $f(x)$ (the single number scoring each choice), and
- a **feasible set** $\mathcal{C}$ (the choices that are allowed) —

written compactly as
$$
\operatorname*{minimize}_{x}\; f(x) \quad\text{subject to}\quad x \in \mathcal{C}.
$$
The **minimum** is the smallest *value* $f$ attains on $\mathcal{C}$; the **minimizer**,
written $\arg\min$, is the *choice* $x^\star$ that attains it. Maximizing $f$ is just
minimizing $-f$, so "optimization" can always be read as minimization. Everything in this
track — every algorithm, every theorem — is about finding $x^\star$ and $f(x^\star)$ for
problems of this exact shape.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The reason we spend a whole article on what looks like
mere notation is that **the entire rest of the series is built on it.** When Regression
says "the best line minimizes squared error," when Inference says "the maximum-likelihood
estimate maximizes the likelihood," when a deep network is "trained," they are all
instances of the one statement in the Core. Get the object right here, once, and those
later sentences stop being slogans and become problems you know how to read.

# Why we are here: the problem behind "find the best" {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **what, precisely, are we after when we
say we want the best of something?** Every definition below is an answer to one piece of
that question. If a piece ever feels like bureaucratic formalism, return here.

## The phrase that hides a problem

"Find the best" is one of the most natural things a human can ask for, and one of the most
slippery. Best *what*? Best *by which measure*? Best *out of which options*? Watch how
much is buried in three ordinary requests:

- *"Draw the best line through these points."* Best how — closest in vertical distance? In
  perpendicular distance? Fewest points missed? The answer changes the line.
- *"Choose the best dose of a drug."* Best for efficacy, ignoring toxicity? Best trade-off
  between the two? And what doses are even permitted — surely not negative, surely not
  above some safety ceiling?
- *"Spend this budget the best way."* Best return, but subject to *not spending more than
  you have*, and perhaps not less than some minimum in each category.

In each case the word "best" silently smuggles in three separate decisions that the asker
never stated. Optimization's first contribution — before any calculus, any algorithm — is
to **drag those three decisions into the open and name them.** Once named, the vague wish
becomes a problem with a definite answer.

## The history in one paragraph

The instinct to optimize is ancient: Euclid (c. 300 BC) showed the shortest path from a
point to a line is the perpendicular, and Heron of Alexandria explained reflection by
light taking the *shortest* path. But optimization became a *method* with the calculus of
Fermat, Newton, and Leibniz in the seventeenth century: Fermat noticed that a smooth
function levels off — its tangent goes flat — exactly at its peaks and troughs, the seed of
"set the derivative to zero" that [OPTM-01] develops in full. The word *program* in
"linear programming" and "mathematical programming" comes from the 1940s, when George
Dantzig and others, working on wartime logistics and resource *programs* (schedules), built
the first systematic algorithms for optimizing under constraints. The modern explosion came
when statistics and machine learning recognized that **fitting a model is solving an
optimization problem** — and suddenly the entire field needed the machinery this track
teaches. The thread connecting all of it, from Heron's light ray to a neural network, is
the single object in the Core box.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is so much of machine learning phrased as optimization in the first place?"* Because
**learning from data is choosing.** You have a family of candidate models indexed by
parameters $x$ (the weights of a network, the coefficients of a regression), and you must
*choose* the parameters that best fit the data. "Best fit" demands a score — a loss
function $f(x)$ measuring how badly parameters $x$ explain the data — and the act of
training *is* the act of minimizing that score. So optimization is not one tool among many
in ML; it is the engine under essentially every supervised method. The objective changes
from problem to problem (squared error, cross-entropy, a penalized likelihood), but the
*shape* of the problem — minimize a score over a set of allowed parameters — never does.
That is why this is a foundational track that the others draw on.
:::

# Intuition before symbols: the landscape {#sec-intuition}

Before any notation, fix the picture that makes every later symbol obvious. Imagine the
objective $f$ as the **height of a landscape**. Every possible choice $x$ is a location on
the ground; the height of the terrain above that location is the score $f(x)$. Minimizing
$f$ means *find the lowest point you can walk to.* Maximizing means find the highest peak.
That single image — a hiker looking for the lowest valley — carries almost everything in
this article, and a surprising amount of the whole track.

The three pieces of the Core read straight off the landscape:

- The **decision variable** is *where you are standing* — the location on the ground you
  are free to move to.
- The **objective** is *the altitude* at your location — the one number the terrain
  assigns to each spot.
- The **feasible set** is *the region you are permitted to walk in* — perhaps a fence rings
  off part of the map, and you must find the lowest point inside the fence, even if a deeper
  valley lies outside it.

@fig-landscape draws this in one dimension, where "the landscape" is just the graph of a
function and "the lowest point you can reach" is a point on that curve.

```{python}
#| label: fig-landscape
#| fig-cap: 'The landscape picture in one dimension. The curve is the objective $f(x)$ — the altitude over each choice $x$. Minimizing $f$ means finding the location of the lowest point on the curve (red). The dashed lines split the two questions this article keeps separate: the horizontal *location* $x^\star$ (the **minimizer**, read off the $x$-axis) and the vertical *altitude* $f(x^\star)$ (the **minimum value**, read off the $y$-axis).'
x = np.linspace(-3.2, 3.2, 400)
f = 0.25 * x**4 - 0.6 * x**3 - 1.5 * x**2 + 2.0 * x + 6.0
# locate the global minimum on this window numerically (for the picture only)
xstar = x[np.argmin(f)]
fstar = f.min()

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, f, color=CURVE, label="objective $f(x)$")
ax.scatter([xstar], [fstar], color=MARK, s=70, zorder=5, label="lowest point")
ax.plot([xstar, xstar], [ax.get_ylim()[0], fstar], color=MARK, ls=":", lw=1.3)
ax.plot([x.min(), xstar], [fstar, fstar], color=MARK, ls=":", lw=1.3)
ax.annotate(r"minimizer $x^\star$", (xstar, ax.get_ylim()[0]),
            xytext=(xstar + 0.2, ax.get_ylim()[0] + 1.0), color=MARK, fontsize=10)
ax.annotate(r"minimum value $f(x^\star)$", (x.min(), fstar),
            xytext=(x.min() + 0.1, fstar + 1.2), color=MARK, fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlabel("choice $x$ (a location)")
ax.set_ylabel("score $f(x)$ (the altitude)")
ax.set_title("Minimizing = finding the lowest point you can reach")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Hold onto this picture. When [OPTM-01] sets a derivative to zero, it is finding where the
terrain goes flat; when [OPTM-06] does gradient descent, it is a hiker stepping downhill in
fog; when [OPTM-13] adds Lagrange multipliers, it is the fence pushing back on the hiker at
the boundary. They are all the same landscape, seen with progressively better equipment.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We now name the three pieces precisely. There is little to *derive* in this article — its
content is *definitions done carefully* — but each definition is chosen to answer a piece
of the "find the best" question, and we say which.

## The decision variable {#sec-decision}

::: {#def-decision}
**Definition (decision variable).** The **decision variable** (or **optimization
variable**) $x$ is the quantity we are free to choose. It ranges over a set of candidate
values; most often $x$ is a single real number ($x \in \mathbb{R}$) or a list of $p$ real
numbers $x = (x_1,\dots,x_p) \in \mathbb{R}^p$, in which case each $x_j$ is a separate knob
we may turn independently.
:::

The decision variable answers *"which of these decisions am I making?"* In a regression fit
it is the coefficients of the line; in a budget problem it is the dollar amount sent to each
category; in training a classifier it is the vector of model weights. The crucial mental
move — the one [REGR-01] calls "the pivot" — is that **the decision variable is the
unknown we solve *for*, not data we are handed.** The data are fixed numbers baked into the
objective; $x$ is what we get to choose.

A note on dimension that matters more than it looks. When $x$ is one number, the landscape
is a curve and we can *see* the whole thing (as in @fig-landscape). When $x$ has two
components, the landscape is a genuine surface — still drawable. When $x$ has a thousand
components (a modest neural network has millions), the landscape lives in a thousand-
dimensional space that *no one can picture*. The entire reason this track grows beyond
[OPTM-01]'s one-variable calculus is that we need tools that keep working when we lose the
ability to draw the picture. But the picture's *logic* — find the lowest reachable point —
never changes, which is why we anchor on it now.

## The objective function {#sec-objective}

::: {#def-objective}
**Definition (objective function).** The **objective function** (also **cost**, **loss**,
or **criterion**) $f$ is a rule that assigns to each candidate choice $x$ a single real
number $f(x)$, the **score** of that choice. Formally $f : \mathcal{C} \to \mathbb{R}$. By
convention we will always *minimize*, so a *lower* score is *better*: $f(x)$ measures how
*bad* the choice $x$ is.
:::

The objective answers *"best by which measure?"* It is the single most consequential
modeling decision in any optimization problem, because **it encodes what you mean by
"best."** Change the objective and you change the answer, even with the same data and the
same allowed choices. [REGR-01] shows this vividly: choosing *squared* error versus
*absolute* error gives two genuinely different "best lines" through the same points. The
objective is where your values, your assumptions, and your priorities get written down as
mathematics — and the rest of the track takes the objective as *given* and asks only how to
minimize it.

Three properties of the objective will dominate later articles, so we name them now even
though we cannot yet exploit them:

- **Single number out.** The objective must return *one* number, even when you care about
  several things. A drug must be both effective *and* safe — two concerns — but to optimize
  you must combine them into one score (say, efficacy minus a penalty for toxicity). The
  art of *scalarizing* competing goals into one objective is a real and subtle craft;
  honest **multi-objective** problems where no single combination is forced are a genuine
  edge of this whole framing, flagged in @sec-edge.
- **Smoothness (or not).** Whether $f$ has a derivative everywhere decides which tools
  apply. The smooth case is [OPTM-01]'s "set the derivative to zero"; the kinked case (like
  the absolute value $|x|$, with its corner at $0$) needs the subgradient machinery of
  [OPTM-05]. We are not using this yet — only flagging that the *shape* of $f$ matters.
- **Convexity (or not).** Whether the landscape is a single bowl or a range of many valleys
  decides whether a local best is *the* best — the dividing line of the entire field,
  developed in [OPTM-03]–[OPTM-04]. We meet its first symptom in @sec-local-global below.

## The feasible set {#sec-feasible}

::: {#def-feasible}
**Definition (feasible set).** The **feasible set** $\mathcal{C}$ is the collection of all
*allowed* choices of $x$. A choice $x$ is **feasible** if $x \in \mathcal{C}$ and
**infeasible** otherwise. When $\mathcal{C}$ is the entire space (every choice allowed),
the problem is **unconstrained**; when $\mathcal{C}$ is a strict subset carved out by
**constraints**, the problem is **constrained**.
:::

The feasible set answers *"best out of which options?"* It is the fence around the
landscape. Constraints usually arrive as **equations and inequalities** that $x$ must
satisfy. For example:

$$
\mathcal{C} = \bigl\{\, x \in \mathbb{R}^p : g_i(x) \le 0 \text{ for all } i,\;\;
h_j(x) = 0 \text{ for all } j \,\bigr\}.
$$ {#eq-feasible}

Read @eq-feasible slowly: it says "$\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all choices $x$ such that
every inequality constraint $g_i(x) \le 0$ holds and every equality constraint $h_j(x) = 0$
holds." A budget constraint "spend no more than \$100" becomes
$g(x) = (\text{total spend}) - 100 \le 0$; a requirement "the probabilities sum to one"
becomes $h(x) = (\sum_k x_k) - 1 = 0$; a non-negativity rule "you cannot buy negative
shares" becomes $g(x) = -x_k \le 0$. The whole **constrained-optimization** branch of this
track ([OPTM-13]–[OPTM-16]) is devoted to honoring fences of exactly this form, and the
*geometry* of feasible sets — what makes a fence "nice" — is the subject of convex sets in
[OPTM-03].

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the difference between an unconstrained and a constrained problem, and why does it
matter so much?"* In an **unconstrained** problem ($\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^p$) you may
choose *any* $x$, so the minimum can only occur where the landscape is *flat* — there is no
fence to lean against, and the entire solution method is "find where the derivative or
gradient vanishes" ([OPTM-01], [OPTM-02]). In a **constrained** problem the minimum can sit
*on the fence*, at a point where the objective is still sloping downhill but the constraint
forbids you from going further. That changes the optimality condition completely: it is no
longer "the slope is zero" but "the slope points straight into the fence" — the geometric
heart of Lagrange multipliers ([OPTM-13]) and the KKT conditions ([OPTM-14]). Recognizing
*which* kind of problem you have is the first thing an interviewer wants to see, because it
selects the entire toolkit.
:::

## Putting it together: the canonical form {#sec-canonical}

With the three pieces named, the full statement of an optimization problem is

$$
\operatorname*{minimize}_{x \in \mathbb{R}^p}\; f(x)
\qquad \text{subject to} \qquad x \in \mathcal{C},
$$ {#eq-canonical}

read aloud as: *"minimize $f$ of $x$, over $x$, subject to $x$ lying in the feasible set
$\mathcal{C}$."* The "$\operatorname*{minimize}$" announces the goal, the "$x$" underneath
announces *what we are choosing* (the decision variable), $f(x)$ is the objective, and the
"subject to" clause states the feasible set. When the constraints are given explicitly as
in @eq-feasible, people often write them out:

$$
\operatorname*{minimize}_{x}\; f(x)
\quad \text{subject to} \quad
g_i(x) \le 0,\;\; h_j(x) = 0.
$$

This is *the* template. Every problem in the track, no matter how elaborate its objective or
how exotic its constraints, fits in @eq-canonical. Learning optimization is learning to
**(1) recognize a vague goal as a problem of this form**, then **(2) solve that problem.**
This article is entirely about step (1); the rest of the track is step (2).

# The two questions a problem asks: `min` versus `argmin` {#sec-min-argmin}

Here is the distinction that separates a careful answer from a sloppy one, and it is asked
in interviews precisely because it is so easy to blur. An optimization problem secretly asks
*two* questions, and they have *two different kinds of answer*.

The **minimum value** is the *best score achievable* — a single number on the altitude
axis. We write it
$$
\min_{x \in \mathcal{C}} f(x) \;=\; \text{the smallest value } f \text{ takes on } \mathcal{C}.
$$
The **minimizer** is the *choice that achieves that best score* — a point on the ground,
living in the same space as $x$. We write it
$$
\arg\min_{x \in \mathcal{C}} f(x) \;=\; \text{the } x^\star \in \mathcal{C}
\text{ at which the minimum is attained, i.e. } f(x^\star) = \min_{x\in\mathcal{C}} f(x).
$$

In the landscape picture (@fig-landscape) the minimum value is *how low* the lowest point
sits (read off the vertical axis); the minimizer is *where on the map* that lowest point is
(read off the horizontal axis). They answer genuinely different questions:

- *"How small can the loss get?"* — wants the **minimum value** (a number).
- *"Which parameters give that smallest loss?"* — wants the **minimizer** (a point).

In data science you almost always care about the **minimizer**: you want the *parameters*
of the best model, not just the number telling you how good the best model is. The fitted
coefficients of a regression are $\arg\min$; the residual sum of squares they achieve is
$\min$. @fig-min-argmin pins the two apart on one curve.

```{python}
#| label: fig-min-argmin
#| fig-cap: '`min` versus `argmin`, on a clean convex objective $f(x) = (x-2)^2 + 1$. The **minimizer** $x^\star = 2$ is the *location* of the lowest point, an answer living on the $x$-axis (green). The **minimum value** $f(x^\star) = 1$ is the *height* there, an answer living on the $y$-axis (red). Two different axes, two different kinds of answer to the same problem.'
x = np.linspace(-1.5, 5.5, 400)
f = (x - 2.0)**2 + 1.0
xstar, fstar = 2.0, 1.0

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, f, color=CURVE, label=r"$f(x) = (x-2)^2 + 1$")
ax.scatter([xstar], [fstar], color=MARK, s=70, zorder=5)
# minimizer: drop to x-axis
ax.plot([xstar, xstar], [0, fstar], color=ACCENT, ls=":", lw=1.6)
ax.scatter([xstar], [0], color=ACCENT, s=55, zorder=5)
ax.annotate(r"$\arg\min f = x^\star = 2$", (xstar, 0),
            xytext=(xstar + 0.2, 1.2), color=ACCENT, fontsize=10)
# minimum value: across to y-axis
ax.plot([-1.5, xstar], [fstar, fstar], color=MARK, ls=":", lw=1.6)
ax.scatter([-1.5], [fstar], color=MARK, s=55, zorder=5)
ax.annotate(r"$\min f = f(x^\star) = 1$", (-1.5, fstar),
            xytext=(-1.3, fstar + 1.4), color=MARK, fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylim(0, 10)
ax.set_xlabel("choice $x$"); ax.set_ylabel("score $f(x)$")
ax.set_title("The value vs. the location")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**`min` is a number; `argmin` is a point — and `argmin` may be empty or may be a whole
set.** Two traps follow. First, never write "$x^\star = \min_x f(x)$": the right-hand side
is the minimum *value*, an altitude, while $x^\star$ is a *location*; equating them is a
type error, like setting a temperature equal to a position. Write $x^\star = \arg\min_x
f(x)$ and $f(x^\star) = \min_x f(x)$. Second, $\arg\min$ need not be a single point: the
constant function $f(x) = 3$ has minimum value $3$ attained *everywhere*, so its $\arg\min$
is the entire line; and a problem can have *no* minimizer at all (@sec-existence). For that
reason $\arg\min$ is, strictly, a *set* of points, and "$x^\star = \arg\min f$" is shorthand
for "$x^\star$ is *a* minimizer," safe only when you know the minimizer is unique — a
guarantee that, as [OPTM-04] shows, comes from *strict convexity*.
:::

## Why maximizing is the same problem upside down {#sec-max}

Half the problems in the wild ask to *maximize* — maximize profit, maximize likelihood,
maximize a reward. We do not need a separate theory for them, because **maximizing $f$ is
identical to minimizing $-f$.** This is not a convention or an approximation; it is an exact
equivalence, and it is worth seeing why in full, because it is the reason this track can
speak only of minimization without loss of generality.

Flip the landscape upside down — replace every altitude $f(x)$ by its negative $-f(x)$.
Peaks become valleys of exactly the same depth and at exactly the same locations. Concretely,
for *any* point $x$ and the highest point $x^\star$,

$$
f(x^\star) \ge f(x) \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathcal{C}
\qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad
-f(x^\star) \le -f(x) \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathcal{C}.
$$

The left statement says "$x^\star$ gives the *largest* value of $f$"; multiplying an
inequality by $-1$ flips its direction (the one algebra step), giving the right statement,
"$x^\star$ gives the *smallest* value of $-f$." The chain of $\Longleftrightarrow$ means the
*same location* $x^\star$ that maximizes $f$ also minimizes $-f$ — the **minimizer is
literally unchanged.** Only the *value* flips sign:

$$
\arg\max_{x\in\mathcal{C}} f(x) = \arg\min_{x\in\mathcal{C}}\,\bigl(-f(x)\bigr),
\qquad
\max_{x\in\mathcal{C}} f(x) = -\min_{x\in\mathcal{C}}\,\bigl(-f(x)\bigr).
$$

@fig-max draws both landscapes at once so the reflection is unmistakable.

```{python}
#| label: fig-max
#| fig-cap: 'Maximizing $f$ is minimizing $-f$. The peak of $f$ (red, up top) sits at the *same horizontal location* $x^\star$ as the trough of $-f$ (blue, below) — reflection through the dashed axis flips altitude but never moves the location. So the maximizer of $f$ and the minimizer of $-f$ are the identical point; only the sign of the value changes. This is why the whole track speaks only of minimization.'
x = np.linspace(-3, 3, 400)
f = np.exp(-0.5 * (x - 0.6)**2) * 4.0   # a single bump (something to maximize)
xstar = x[np.argmax(f)]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.0, ls="--")
ax.plot(x, f, color=MARK, label=r"$f(x)$  (maximize this)")
ax.plot(x, -f, color=CURVE, label=r"$-f(x)$  (minimize this)")
ax.scatter([xstar], [f.max()], color=MARK, s=60, zorder=5)
ax.scatter([xstar], [-f.max()], color=CURVE, s=60, zorder=5)
ax.plot([xstar, xstar], [-f.max(), f.max()], color=ACCENT, ls=":", lw=1.4)
ax.annotate(r"same $x^\star$", (xstar, 0), xytext=(xstar + 0.25, 0.4),
            color=ACCENT, fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlabel("choice $x$"); ax.set_ylabel("score")
ax.set_title("A maximum of $f$ is a minimum of $-f$ at the same place")
ax.legend(loc="lower left", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"The statistician maximizes the log-likelihood, but the optimizer minimizes a loss. Are
they doing different things?"* No — they are doing the same thing with opposite sign
conventions. Maximizing the log-likelihood $\ell(x)$ is exactly minimizing the **negative
log-likelihood** $-\ell(x)$, and "NLL" is precisely the loss that machine-learning code
minimizes. So when [REGR-01] shows that the least-squares line *is* the maximum-likelihood
line under Gaussian noise, and the code minimizes squared error, there is no contradiction:
minimizing the squared-error loss and maximizing the Gaussian likelihood are the same
problem, related by exactly the $f \leftrightarrow -f$ flip above. Being fluent in this
translation is the difference between thinking these are two fields and knowing they are
one. (The maximization-flavored algorithm, *gradient ascent*, is just gradient descent on
$-f$; it is named explicitly in [OPTM-06].)
:::

# Local versus global: a valley is not always *the* valley {#sec-local-global}

The landscape picture warns of a danger the smooth phrase "the lowest point" papers over.
A real terrain can have *several* valleys. The lowest point in the small dip beside you is
a perfectly good *local* answer — you cannot do better without first climbing out — but it
need not be the *global* lowest point on the whole map. This distinction is the single most
important caveat in all of optimization, and we name it precisely.

::: {#def-local-global}
**Definition (local and global minimizers).** A feasible point $x^\star \in \mathcal{C}$ is
a **global minimizer** if no feasible point does better:
$$
f(x^\star) \le f(x) \quad \text{for every } x \in \mathcal{C}.
$$
It is a **local minimizer** if it merely beats its *neighbors* — there is some radius
$r > 0$ such that
$$
f(x^\star) \le f(x) \quad \text{for every feasible } x \text{ within distance } r \text{ of } x^\star.
$$
Every global minimizer is local (if you beat everyone, you beat your neighbors), but a local
minimizer need not be global.
:::

Read the two definitions side by side. The *global* condition quantifies over **every**
feasible point — it is a statement about the entire map. The *local* condition quantifies
only over points **within distance $r$** — a statement about a small neighborhood. The
gap between "best among my neighbors" and "best anywhere" is exactly the gap between a dip
you happened to wander into and the true bottom. @fig-local-global shows a landscape with
both, and it is the picture to keep in mind every time an algorithm "converges."

```{python}
#| label: fig-local-global
#| fig-cap: "A landscape with more than one valley. The point on the right (orange) is a genuine **local** minimizer — every nearby point is higher, so a downhill-walking algorithm that arrives here gets stuck. But it is *not* **global**: the valley on the left (red) is strictly deeper. An algorithm that only ever steps downhill cannot tell these apart from inside the right-hand dip; distinguishing them requires either luck, restarts, or the structural guarantee of convexity ([OPTM-04]) that *forbids* a second valley."
x = np.linspace(-3.5, 4.0, 500)
f = 0.5 * np.sin(1.6 * x) + 0.12 * (x - 0.4)**2 + 0.04 * x
i_glob = np.argmin(f)
# find a local min on the right region that is not the global one
mask = x > 1.0
i_loc_rel = np.argmin(f[mask])
i_loc = np.arange(len(x))[mask][i_loc_rel]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, f, color=CURVE, label="objective $f(x)$")
ax.scatter([x[i_glob]], [f[i_glob]], color=MARK, s=75, zorder=5,
           label="global minimizer")
ax.scatter([x[i_loc]], [f[i_loc]], color="#e07a1f", s=75, zorder=5,
           label="a local (non-global) minimizer")
ax.set_xlabel("choice $x$"); ax.set_ylabel("score $f(x)$")
ax.set_title("Local minima are traps a downhill walker cannot escape")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Why dwell on this in an introductory article? Because it sets up the *organizing question*
of the entire track. Almost every algorithm we will build — gradient descent, Newton's
method, SGD — is, at heart, a downhill walker: it can find a *local* minimizer, but on a
landscape with many valleys it has no way to know whether it found *the* one. There are only
two escapes, and the track is structured around them:

- **Restructure the problem** so it has only one valley. A **convex** objective is exactly
  one whose landscape is a single bowl — no second valley to get trapped in — and for it
  *every* local minimizer is automatically global. This is "the whole ballgame" of
  [OPTM-04], and the reason convexity is treated as a dividing line: on the convex side, a
  local answer *is* the answer; on the non-convex side, it generally is not.
- **Accept local minima and manage them.** For genuinely non-convex problems (training a
  deep network is the headline example) we cannot guarantee the global optimum, so we use
  many random starts, momentum, and noise to escape shallow traps — the practical content of
  [OPTM-08], [OPTM-11], and [OPTM-12].

For *this* article the lesson is only that the word "minimum" hides two meanings, and that
which one an algorithm delivers is never automatic. We flag it now; the track spends a dozen
articles cashing it out.

# When there is no answer at all: existence {#sec-existence}

We have quietly assumed a lowest point *exists*. It need not. This is the first honest edge
of the framing — the place where the neat phrase "minimize $f$ over $\mathcal{C}$" describes
a question with **no answer**, and recognizing those cases is part of mastery, not a
footnote to it.

There are two distinct ways a minimizer can fail to exist, and the landscape picture makes
both obvious.

**Failure one: the floor keeps dropping (unbounded below).** If the terrain falls forever as
you walk in some direction, there is no lowest point — for any altitude you name, you can get
lower. The objective $f(x) = x$ over all of $\mathbb{R}$ has no minimum: as $x \to -\infty$,
$f \to -\infty$, so no single $x^\star$ achieves the smallest value, because *there is no
smallest value*. The plain English diagnosis: **an unconstrained problem whose objective
can be driven to $-\infty$ has no minimizer.** Constraints often cure this by fencing off
the runaway direction.

**Failure two: the bottom is missing (the set is open, or the infimum is not attained).**
This one is subtler and is the reason mathematicians distinguish the *infimum* (the greatest
lower bound — the altitude the terrain *approaches*) from the *minimum* (a value actually
*attained* at some point). Consider minimizing $f(x) = x$ over the **open** interval
$\mathcal{C} = (0, 1)$ — all numbers strictly between $0$ and $1$, with the endpoints *not*
included. You can make $f$ as close to $0$ as you like ($x = 0.001$, then $x = 0.000001$,
…), so the values *approach* $0$ — but $0$ itself is not in $\mathcal{C}$, and *every*
feasible point has another feasible point below it. There is a greatest lower bound (the
infimum, $0$) but **no feasible point attains it**, so the minimum does not exist. The
problem has an infimum but no minimizer. @fig-existence draws both failures.

```{python}
#| label: fig-existence
#| fig-cap: 'Two ways a minimizer fails to exist. **Left:** $f(x)=x$ on all of $\mathbb{R}$ is *unbounded below* — the curve falls forever, so there is no lowest point. **Right:** $f(x)=x$ on the *open* interval $(0,1)$ (hollow endpoints) approaches the value $0$ but never reaches it — the infimum (dashed green) is $0$, yet no feasible point attains it, so there is no minimizer. In both cases the phrase ''minimize $f$'' describes a question with no answer.'
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.7))

# Left: unbounded below
xx = np.linspace(-4, 4, 200)
axL.plot(xx, xx, color=CURVE)
axL.annotate("falls forever", (-3.5, -3.5), xytext=(-3.3, -1.2),
             color=MARK, fontsize=10,
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=MARK))
axL.set_title(r"Unbounded below: $f(x)=x$ on $\mathbb{R}$")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$f(x)$")

# Right: open interval, infimum not attained
xo = np.linspace(0.001, 0.999, 200)
axR.plot(xo, xo, color=CURVE)
axR.scatter([0, 1], [0, 1], facecolors="white", edgecolors=CURVE,
            s=70, zorder=5, linewidth=1.8)  # hollow = excluded endpoints
axR.axhline(0, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.4)
axR.annotate("infimum 0,\nnever attained", (0, 0), xytext=(0.18, 0.35),
             color=ACCENT, fontsize=10)
axR.set_xlim(-0.15, 1.15); axR.set_ylim(-0.2, 1.1)
axR.set_title(r"Open set: $f(x)=x$ on $(0,1)$")
axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_ylabel("$f(x)$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

So when *is* a minimizer guaranteed to exist? Both failures point at the cure. Failure one
is ruled out if the objective cannot run off to $-\infty$; failure two is ruled out if the
feasible set *includes* its own boundary, so an approached value is actually reached. The
classical theorem packages exactly this.

::: {#thm-weierstrass}
**Theorem (the existence guarantee, at the floor's level).** If the feasible set
$\mathcal{C}$ is **closed** (it includes its own boundary — no missing edges like the open
interval) and **bounded** (it fits inside some finite region — it does not run off to
infinity), and the objective $f$ is **continuous** (its graph has no jumps), then $f$
attains both a minimum and a maximum on $\mathcal{C}$: a minimizer $x^\star \in \mathcal{C}$
exists.
:::

::: {.proof}
A full proof belongs to a real-analysis course and rests on the technical notion of
*compactness* (in $\mathbb{R}^p$, "closed and bounded"), which sits above this track's
Calculus-I floor — so we **flag it as an imported prerequisite** rather than derive it
here, and give the honest *intuition* that the floor does support. "Bounded" forbids
Failure one: the terrain cannot fall forever because the region you may walk in is finite,
so the altitudes you can reach are themselves bounded below, pinning down a greatest lower
bound (an infimum). "Closed" forbids Failure two: the open-interval pathology happened
precisely because the value $0$ was approached at a *missing* boundary point; a closed set
contains its boundary, so any value the function approaches along a sequence of feasible
points is actually achieved *at* a feasible point — the limit point is itself in
$\mathcal{C}$. "Continuous" is what links the two: it guarantees that the altitude
approached along such a sequence equals the altitude *at* the limiting location, so the
infimum is attained there and is a genuine minimum. The slogan worth memorizing is **"a
continuous function on a closed, bounded set attains its extremes."** $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-note title="What we imported, and where it lives"}
This is the one place the article reaches above its Calculus-I floor. The full statement
and proof of @thm-weierstrass — the **Extreme Value Theorem**, and its general form the
*Weierstrass theorem* — rest on **compactness** and the behavior of sequences, which are
real-analysis machinery the Core Sample series treats as a named prerequisite. We imported
only the *statement* and gave the intuition the floor supports, so that the logic of "when
does a best choice exist" could run end to end. Everything else in this article stays at the
floor.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Your training loss decreased every epoch but never seemed to settle — what could be going
on?"* The two failure modes of this section are live suspects, dressed in machine-learning
clothes. If the loss can be driven *down without bound* — a model with an unpenalized weight
that can grow forever while the loss keeps shrinking — you have **Failure one**, an
objective unbounded below, and the standard cure is to *add a constraint or a penalty* (a
regularizer like $\lambda\|x\|^2$) that fences off the runaway direction and restores a
finite minimum. If instead the *optimal value is approached but never attained* — for
instance a perfectly separable classification problem where pushing the weights ever larger
makes the logistic loss ever smaller without limit — you have **Failure two**, an infimum
that lives "at infinity" and is never reached, again cured by regularization that pulls the
boundary back in. Knowing that "the loss won't settle" is often an *existence* problem, not
a tuning problem, is exactly the practitioner judgment this track is for.
:::

# Worked framings: reading real goals into the canonical form {#sec-examples}

The skill this article teaches is *translation*: taking a goal stated in plain language and
writing it as @eq-canonical — naming the decision variable, the objective, and the feasible
set. We do that now for three honest examples drawn from the rest of the series, ending with
one at the edge of the framing where the translation strains. We do not *solve* them — the
methods are later articles — we only put each into the canonical form, which is the step
this article owns.

## Framing 1 — Least squares as "minimize total squared error" {#sec-ls}

This is the example the Regression track ([REGR-01]) is built on, and the one [OPTM-02] will
solve. We have $n$ data pairs $(t_i, y_i)$ — say, waiting time $t_i$ and eruption duration
$y_i$ for a geyser — and we want the *best straight line* $y \approx \beta_0 + \beta_1 t$
through them. Translate:

- **Decision variable:** the line is determined by its intercept and slope, so
  $x = (\beta_0, \beta_1) \in \mathbb{R}^2$. *That* is what we choose — not the data, which
  are fixed.
- **Objective:** "best" means smallest total squared vertical gap (the choice [REGR-01]
  justifies in full),
  $$
  f(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \bigl(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 t_i\bigr)^2.
  $$
- **Feasible set:** any line is allowed, so $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$ — this is an
  **unconstrained** problem.

In canonical form: $\operatorname*{minimize}_{(\beta_0,\beta_1)\in\mathbb{R}^2}
\sum_i (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 t_i)^2$. @fig-ls-framing shows the objective as a landscape
over the plane of candidate lines — a single bowl, so its one valley is *the* valley
(convex, foreshadowing [OPTM-04]).

```{python}
#| label: fig-ls-framing
#| fig-cap: 'Least squares as an optimization problem over the *plane of candidate lines*. Each location $(\beta_0,\beta_1)$ is one possible line; its altitude is the total squared error that line makes against the data. The landscape is a single convex bowl, so there is exactly one lowest point (red) — the best-fitting line. This is the picture [OPTM-02] solves by setting the gradient to zero.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
t = np.linspace(1, 9, 12)
y = 2.0 + 0.8 * t + rng.normal(0, 1.0, size=t.size)

b0g = np.linspace(-1, 5, 60)
b1g = np.linspace(-0.4, 2.0, 60)
B0, B1 = np.meshgrid(b0g, b1g)
S = np.zeros_like(B0)
for i in range(B0.shape[0]):
    for j in range(B0.shape[1]):
        pred = B0[i, j] + B1[i, j] * t
        S[i, j] = np.sum((y - pred)**2)

# the minimizing line, for the marker (closed form, used only to draw the dot)
tb, yb = t.mean(), y.mean()
b1s = np.sum((t - tb) * (y - yb)) / np.sum((t - tb)**2)
b0s = yb - b1s * tb
Sstar = np.sum((y - (b0s + b1s * t))**2)

from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6.4, 4.6))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
ax.plot_surface(B0, B1, S, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.85, linewidth=0, antialiased=True)
ax.scatter([b0s], [b1s], [Sstar], color=MARK, s=70, depthshade=False)
ax.text(b0s, b1s, Sstar + 25, "best line", color=MARK)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\beta_0$ (intercept)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$\beta_1$ (slope)")
ax.set_zlabel(r"$f(\beta_0,\beta_1)$")
ax.set_title("Squared error over the plane of lines")
ax.view_init(elev=28, azim=-128)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Framing 2 — A constrained budget allocation {#sec-budget}

Now a problem where the fence does real work. An investor has \$1 of capital to split across
$p$ assets; let $x_k \ge 0$ be the fraction placed in asset $k$. A standard objective (the
Markowitz idea) trades expected return against risk: minimize the portfolio's *variance* — a
single number measuring how much the portfolio's value jitters, which a long-only investor
wants small — while the allocations form a valid, fully-invested, long-only portfolio.
Concretely, the variance is a weighted sum
$$
f(x) \;=\; \sum_{k}\sum_{m} x_k\,x_m\,\Sigma_{km},
$$
where $\Sigma_{km}$ is a fixed number describing how assets $k$ and $m$ move together (their
*covariance*, a quantity owned by the Probability track and taken as given here). Each term
multiplies *two* of our chosen fractions, so $f$ is **quadratic** in $x$ — a bowl-shaped
score, the simplest objective beyond a straight line. (In matrix shorthand this same sum is
written $x^\top \Sigma x$, the **quadratic form** that [OPTM-02] and the Geometry track
develop; we only need its plain-English reading — "a weighted sum of products of the
allocations" — to *frame* the problem here.) Translate:

- **Decision variable:** $x = (x_1,\dots,x_p) \in \mathbb{R}^p$, the fractions invested.
- **Objective:** $f(x) = \sum_{k}\sum_{m} x_k x_m \Sigma_{km}$, the portfolio variance
  (smaller is safer).
- **Feasible set:** the constraints are *fully invested*
  $\sum_k x_k = 1$ (an equality $h(x) = \sum_k x_k - 1 = 0$) and *no short selling*
  $x_k \ge 0$ for each $k$ (inequalities $g_k(x) = -x_k \le 0$). So
  $$
  \mathcal{C} = \Bigl\{\, x \in \mathbb{R}^p : \textstyle\sum_k x_k = 1,\;\; x_k \ge 0 \,\Bigr\},
  $$
  the **probability simplex** — a genuinely constrained set.

This is exactly the shape of @eq-canonical with both equality and inequality constraints. It
cannot be solved by "set the derivative to zero" alone, because the minimum may sit *on the
fence* — at an allocation where lowering one asset's variance contribution would require a
negative position the constraint forbids. That is the situation Lagrange multipliers and the
KKT conditions ([OPTM-13]–[OPTM-14]) are built for. @fig-budget pictures the feasible set for
$p = 3$ and a sample objective contour landing on it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-budget
#| fig-cap: 'A constrained problem for three assets. The feasible set (green triangle) is the probability simplex $\{x_1+x_2+x_3=1,\ x_k\ge 0\}$ — every valid fully-invested, long-only portfolio, shown in the $(x_1,x_2)$ plane with $x_3 = 1 - x_1 - x_2$ implied. The background shading is the objective (portfolio variance); the constrained minimum (red) is the lowest-variance point *inside the triangle*, which may differ from the unconstrained minimum that ignores the fence. Honoring such fences is the constrained-optimization branch of the track.'
# Objective: a simple quadratic in (x1, x2) standing in for portfolio variance.
g = np.linspace(-0.1, 1.1, 300)
X1, X2 = np.meshgrid(g, g)
# variance-like bowl with a minimum near (0.55, 0.15) (outside? inside? — illustrative)
F = 1.6 * (X1 - 0.55)**2 + 1.1 * (X2 - 0.15)**2 + 0.7 * (X1 - 0.55) * (X2 - 0.15)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 5.0))
cs = ax.contourf(X1, X2, F, levels=18, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.55)
# the simplex region x1>=0, x2>=0, x1+x2<=1
tri = plt.Polygon([(0, 0), (1, 0), (0, 1)], closed=True,
                  facecolor=ACCENT, alpha=0.30, edgecolor=ACCENT, lw=2.0,
                  label="feasible set (simplex)")
ax.add_patch(tri)
# constrained minimizer: search F on a fine grid restricted to the simplex
pts = []
for a in np.linspace(0, 1, 200):
    for b in np.linspace(0, 1 - a, 200):
        pts.append((a, b))
pts = np.array(pts)
vals = 1.6 * (pts[:, 0] - 0.55)**2 + 1.1 * (pts[:, 1] - 0.15)**2 \
       + 0.7 * (pts[:, 0] - 0.55) * (pts[:, 1] - 0.15)
xc = pts[np.argmin(vals)]
ax.scatter([xc[0]], [xc[1]], color=MARK, s=80, zorder=6,
           label="constrained minimum")
ax.set_xlim(-0.1, 1.1); ax.set_ylim(-0.1, 1.1)
ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$  (asset 1 share)")
ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$  (asset 2 share)")
ax.set_title("Minimize variance over the simplex")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.95, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Framing 3 — Maximum likelihood as maximization rewritten as minimization {#sec-mle}

The Inference track repeatedly says "estimate the parameter by *maximizing* the likelihood."
By @sec-max that is a minimization in disguise, and seeing the translation is the point.
Suppose we observe counts and model them with a one-parameter distribution whose parameter
$\theta > 0$ controls the rate. The **likelihood** $L(\theta)$ is the probability the model
assigns to the data we saw, as a function of $\theta$; the maximum-likelihood estimate is the
$\theta$ that makes the observed data most probable. Translate:

- **Decision variable:** $x = \theta$, with the natural domain $\theta > 0$.
- **Objective:** we *maximize* $L(\theta)$, equivalently *maximize* the log-likelihood
  $\ell(\theta) = \ln L(\theta)$ (logs do not move the location of a peak, since $\ln$ is
  increasing — the same step [REGR-01] uses), equivalently **minimize the negative
  log-likelihood** $f(\theta) = -\ell(\theta)$.
- **Feasible set:** $\mathcal{C} = (0, \infty)$, the allowed parameter values.

So "maximize the likelihood over $\theta > 0$" becomes the canonical
$\operatorname*{minimize}_{\theta > 0}\ \bigl(-\ell(\theta)\bigr)$ — and note the feasible
set $(0,\infty)$ is *open*, exactly the @sec-existence Failure-two danger: if the negative
log-likelihood kept decreasing as $\theta \to 0^+$ or $\theta \to \infty$, no estimate would
exist. @fig-mle shows the flip and the safe interior optimum.

```{python}
#| label: fig-mle
#| fig-cap: 'Maximum likelihood as minimization. The log-likelihood $\ell(\theta)$ (red) is *maximized* at $\theta^\star$; the negative log-likelihood $-\ell(\theta)$ (blue) is *minimized* at the identical $\theta^\star$ (dashed line). The optimizer is the same parameter either way — only the sign of the value differs. The optimum sits in the interior of the allowed range $\theta>0$, so a minimizer exists; were the curve to fall forever toward an edge, it would not (the @sec-existence caution).'
theta = np.linspace(0.3, 6.0, 400)
# illustrative concave log-likelihood with an interior peak
ell = -1.0 * (np.log(theta) - np.log(2.2))**2 * 3.0 - 0.2 * theta + 2.0
tstar = theta[np.argmax(ell)]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(theta, ell, color=MARK, label=r"log-likelihood $\ell(\theta)$ (maximize)")
ax.plot(theta, -ell, color=CURVE, label=r"$-\ell(\theta)$ (minimize)")
ax.axvline(tstar, color=ACCENT, ls=":", lw=1.6)
ax.scatter([tstar], [ell.max()], color=MARK, s=60, zorder=5)
ax.scatter([tstar], [-ell.max()], color=CURVE, s=60, zorder=5)
ax.annotate(r"same $\theta^\star$", (tstar, 0), xytext=(tstar + 0.2, 0.5),
            color=ACCENT, fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlabel(r"parameter $\theta$"); ax.set_ylabel("score")
ax.set_title("MLE: maximize $\\ell$ = minimize $-\\ell$")
ax.legend(loc="lower center", framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Framing 4 (the edge) — when "find the best" resists a single objective {#sec-edge}

Every framing above quietly assumed a *single* number could score each choice. The honest
edge of this whole formulation is the case where it cannot. Suppose you are designing a
treatment and you care about **two** things at once: maximize efficacy *and* minimize
toxicity. There is no God-given exchange rate between "one more unit of efficacy" and "one
more unit of toxicity," so there is no single objective $f$ that everyone agrees on — and
the canonical form @eq-canonical, which demands *one* objective, does not directly apply.

This is a **multi-objective** problem, and it has no single minimizer; instead it has a
whole frontier of *non-dominated* (Pareto-optimal) choices — options where you cannot
improve one goal without sacrificing the other. @fig-pareto shows that frontier. There are
two honest responses, and naming them is the practitioner judgment this article is meant to
build:

- **Scalarize.** Pick weights and collapse the goals into one objective, e.g.
  $f(x) = -\text{efficacy}(x) + \lambda\,\text{toxicity}(x)$, restoring the canonical form —
  but *now the weight $\lambda$ is a value judgment you have imported*, and different
  $\lambda$ trace out different points on the frontier. This is exactly how a regularization
  parameter ($\lambda\|x\|^2$) enters later articles: it is a scalarization of "fit the data
  well" against "keep the model simple."
- **Constrain one, optimize the other.** Fix an acceptable toxicity ceiling and maximize
  efficacy subject to it — turning the second objective into a *constraint* and landing back
  in the constrained canonical form of @sec-budget. This is often the most defensible move,
  because the ceiling is a concrete, stateable policy.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pareto
#| fig-cap: "The edge of the single-objective framing. With two competing goals (here: low toxicity *and* high efficacy, both wanted), no single point is 'best' — the grey cloud of options has a **Pareto frontier** (red) of non-dominated choices, each of which trades one goal for the other. The canonical form needs *one* objective, so to use the rest of this track you must either *scalarize* (pick a weight, collapsing the frontier to one chosen point) or *constrain* one goal and optimize the other. There is no purely mathematical way to pick a single point on the frontier — that choice is a value judgment."
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
tox = rng.uniform(0.05, 1.0, size=220)
# efficacy is, on average, higher when toxicity is higher (a real trade-off), plus noise
eff = 0.4 + 0.7 * np.sqrt(tox) - 0.25 * tox**2 + rng.normal(0, 0.06, size=tox.size)

# Pareto frontier: points not dominated (we want LOW tox, HIGH eff)
order = np.argsort(tox)
ts, es = tox[order], eff[order]
frontier_mask = np.zeros(len(ts), dtype=bool)
best_eff = -np.inf
for i in range(len(ts)):           # scanning toward higher toxicity
    if es[i] > best_eff:           # a new highest-efficacy-so-far is non-dominated
        frontier_mask[i] = True
        best_eff = es[i]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter(tox, eff, color=GREY, s=18, alpha=0.55, label="candidate treatments")
ax.plot(ts[frontier_mask], es[frontier_mask], color=MARK, marker="o",
        ms=4, lw=1.8, label="Pareto frontier")
ax.set_xlabel("toxicity  (want low $\\rightarrow$)")
ax.set_ylabel("efficacy  (want high $\\uparrow$)")
ax.set_title("Two goals, no single 'best': the Pareto frontier")
ax.legend(loc="lower right", framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The hardest part of optimization is often choosing the objective, and that step is not
itself an optimization problem.** This track will give you powerful machinery to minimize
*any* $f$ you hand it — but it cannot tell you whether $f$ encodes what you actually want.
A model that minimizes a misspecified loss will faithfully optimize the *wrong thing*: an
objective that ignores fairness will cheerfully find the most accurate-but-unfair model; a
scalarization with a badly chosen $\lambda$ will sit at a point on the Pareto frontier no
stakeholder would have picked. The mathematics is value-neutral; the *choice of objective
and constraints* is where your judgment lives. Optimization makes "find the best" precise —
it does not absolve you of deciding what "best" should mean.
:::

# When this framing applies, and when it does not {#sec-applicability}

Pulling the threads together into the practitioner judgment the series is for.

**Use the optimization framing when** your goal can be honestly written as *one* scalar
score over a *well-defined* set of allowed choices: fitting a model to data (the objective
is a loss), allocating a resource (the objective is cost or return, the set is a budget),
calibrating a system (the objective is a discrepancy you want small). The overwhelming
majority of supervised machine learning, statistical estimation, and engineering design
lives comfortably here, which is why this track is load-bearing for the rest of the series.

**Be careful — or reach for something else — when:**

- **The goals genuinely conflict and no exchange rate is given** (@sec-edge). Force them
  into one objective only with an explicit, defensible scalarization, or constrain all but
  one. Pretending a value judgment is a mathematical fact is the most common abuse of the
  framing.
- **The objective is not what you actually care about.** Minimizing training loss is a
  *proxy* for generalizing to new data; optimizing the proxy too hard (overfitting) can make
  the real goal *worse*. Recognizing the gap between the objective you can write down and the
  outcome you want is the entire reason regularization and validation exist.
- **A best choice may not exist** (@sec-existence). Unbounded or open problems have no
  minimizer; the fix is usually a constraint or a penalty, and noticing that "it won't
  converge" is an existence problem rather than a tuning problem is real expertise.
- **The decision space is discrete and combinatorial** (which subset of features, which
  routing of a network). The smooth-landscape calculus this track is built on does not
  directly apply; that is the separate world of integer and combinatorial optimization, where
  one reaches for branch-and-bound, dynamic programming, or specialized solvers. This track
  is about *continuous* optimization, and it says so honestly.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Give me an example of a real problem that does **not** fit the standard optimization
framing, and say why."* A clean answer names a *discrete* or *multi-objective* case and
explains the mismatch. For instance: *"Choosing which subset of 50 features to include in a
model is a combinatorial problem — the decision variable lives in a finite set of $2^{50}$
subsets, not a continuous $\mathbb{R}^p$, so 'set the gradient to zero' has no meaning and
the convex-optimization toolkit doesn't apply; you'd reach for combinatorial methods or a
continuous relaxation like the lasso instead."* Or: *"Designing a drug to be both effective
and non-toxic has no single objective — it's multi-objective, with a Pareto frontier rather
than one optimum, so you must either scalarize with a chosen weight or constrain one goal."*
Showing you know the *boundary* of the framing, not just how to operate inside it, is what
separates a graduate-level answer from a recipe-follower's.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
This is the **vocabulary article** — the front door. You now have the one object the whole
track manipulates: $\operatorname*{minimize}_{x\in\mathcal{C}} f(x)$, with its decision
variable, objective, and feasible set; the $\min$/$\arg\min$ split; max-as-min; the
local/global distinction; and the honest cases where no minimizer exists. From here the
track begins to *solve*:

- **[OPTM-01] *One-Dimensional Minimization: the Derivative Test*** — the first method.
  When $x$ is a single number and $f$ is smooth, Calculus I locates the minimum by setting
  $f'(x) = 0$, with the full *why* (a nonzero slope means you can still go downhill) and the
  honest cases where the derivative test alone lies. This is the seed of everything.
- **[OPTM-02] *Minimizing in Many Variables: $\nabla f = 0$*** — the same logic when $x$ is
  a vector: stationary points, the Hessian test, and the OLS normal equations of
  @sec-ls solved at last.
- **The two spines.** *Convexity* ([OPTM-03]–[OPTM-05]) answers the question raised in
  @sec-local-global — *when is a local minimum the global one?* *Descent* ([OPTM-06] onward)
  answers *how do we reach a minimum when no formula exists?* They reconverge at the
  algorithms that train real models.
- **Constrained optimization** ([OPTM-13]–[OPTM-16]) honors the fences of @sec-budget; the
  *calculus of variations* ([OPTM-17]) extends the whole framing from choosing a *point* to
  choosing an entire *function*.

And the outward links: this article is what Regression, Inference, Classification, and
Unsupervised Learning all point back to when they say "fit the model by minimizing *this*."
The sentence now has a precise meaning, and the rest of this track is how to carry it out.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The classical roots.** Fermat's *Methodus ad disquirendam maximam et minimam* (c. 1636)
  is the origin of "the tangent is flat at an extremum," the idea [OPTM-01] formalizes.
  Heron of Alexandria's *Catoptrica* argued reflection follows the shortest path — optimization
  as a *principle of nature*, a thread that runs through Fermat's principle of least time and
  the calculus of variations ([OPTM-17]).
- **The modern texts.** Boyd & Vandenberghe, *Convex Optimization* (2004, free online), is
  the standard reference for the canonical form, feasible sets, and everything in the
  convexity and constrained spines; Nocedal & Wright, *Numerical Optimization* (2006), is the
  reference for the descent and Newton algorithms. Both open, as we did, by fixing exactly the
  object in the Core.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *How do we actually find $x^\star$?* —
  every remaining article in the track. *When is a local minimum guaranteed global?* —
  convexity, [OPTM-04]. *What exactly is "closed and bounded," and why does compactness force
  a minimum?* — a real-analysis prerequisite the series treats as imported (the
  Weierstrass/Extreme-Value theorem behind @thm-weierstrass). *What if the decision variable
  is a whole function, not a vector?* — the calculus of variations, [OPTM-17].
